# Transcrição: 07-Referral Rewards Brainstorm

- **Fonte:** 07-Referral Rewards Brainstorm
- **Duração:** 12:14
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:04]

So let's talk about the eight best ethical bribes you can use in your business. And when I say bribe, you may be thinking neat, that sounds a little dirty, that sounds a little something under the table, maybe something you wouldn't necessarily want to do. But an ethical bribe is just that. It's ethical. It's not bribing people to do something that's illegal or nefarious or anything like that. It's simply offering a way to reward your customers for helping to bring customers to you so that you can grow your business, you can create a bigger impact, and more people can get the outcome that you're providing to the market. So the first ethical bribe, and it's one of my absolute favorites, is group discounts. Now a group discount is simply when you offer a discount when someone provides a referral that results in some type of a purchase. An example of this might be where you offer the opportunity to get your product or offer for free when they provide three referrals that make a purchase. Now a hot tip to consider here is anytime someone says that they can't afford your product or service, you offer them a group discount. One of my mentors really likes to say that anytime someone says, I would love to move forward with your product, or I would love to move forward, or I would love to purchase the seminar, but I just can't afford it right now. It's outside of my price range. The first thing they'll say is great, you're hired, because I just hired you to go out and get three other people to bring with you to this event, and when you do, I'll offer you the event for complete free. So that's a way that you can leverage group discounts to actually take care of that no because they couldn't afford it and make it affordable for some of the tryout. The next way is the client loyalty ladder, and the client loyalty ladder is another one of my favorites, and it's something that you've probably seen executed before, but you may not be thinking about it in the way that it relates specifically to referrals. And a client loyalty ladder is to simply create a tiered system that recognizes your customers and provides them with the opportunity to give you referrals. An example of this might be if they give one referral, they reach the bronze level. If they provide three referrals, they might reach the silver level, and if they provide five referrals, they might reach a gold level. You can set up any type of tiered system that works for you, but basically the general idea and concept behind it is that you have a tiered system that recognizes people for actually referring business to you. If you have a large community or a group, this gives value to the people that are providing referrals. It gives them trust, and it gives them the ability to actually say that they're out there providing referrals to you in your course, in your community, and things like that. So when you do that, a hot tip that I recommend is that you actually leverage Canva, and you can create digital badges to share their status. That way when they're in the group and they have their profile picture, they might have that little silver, bronze, or gold star or emblem or whatever it might be, and it gives

## [03:04 - 06:10]

them a little bit of that status increase in the group to help them be recognized for giving those additional referrals. The other thing that you can do when developing this is you can look at ways that you can leverage Gigi to create your client loyalty ladder. So think of ways that you could actually leverage a tool like Gigi and just ask the question, how would you help me to develop a client loyalty ladder in my business and see what response you get? The third ethical bribe you can do is a prize or a giveaway. We've all seen these in different places as well because it's very similar to a raffle. You could consider giving prizes away based on customer referrals. Each referral counts as a chance to win, and then you give away whatever prize it is that you've come up with. An example might be that you share, that you're in a charitable mood, and you're going to give X amount of dollars away for someone's favorite charity. Each referral that they give counts as a chance to win. It's both a win for the charity, it's a win for the customer because they're giving away to one of their favorite charities, and it's a win for you because you get referrals in the end. Another thing to consider is if you don't have money to invest, consider providing a free seat on your coaching program or even including something specific from your program or your offer. An idea for you here is to simply write down different ideas where you could, different prizes you could do, different giveaways, different raffles that you could use in your business. Take those notes, write those ideas down, store them somewhere, and then the next time you have the opportunity, create this as one of your referral generating engines. The fourth way that you can generate referrals from ethical bribes is actually shout outs and mentions. People love being shouted out inside of groups and they love being put on a pedestal, so to speak, and people sharing the actual mention or the comment around them. One way that you could do this is you could actually share the referral that was given or you could share the success that someone that was a referral actually got. An example would be that when you close a sale that came in from a referral, welcome the new person into your actual community and shout out the person who actually referred them. A hot tip that I have here is share a success story that came from one of your referrals. Highlight the fact that without that person's referral, that person's success may never have happened. Highlight the fact that those referrals are necessary to bring people into the group so that you can create the impact, get the outcome, and share that success all around. The fifth type of ethical bribe is a percentage discount per referral. This is one of my absolute favorites because I actually did this in a SaaS company that I worked with where we put a referral process in place where for each customer they got a certain percentage off for the purchase of the actual software license that they had. In the case that we had, we offered 1% discount for each customer who was referred.

## [06:10 - 09:10]

At first, when I shared this idea, the owner of the company, the founder, was freaking out because he was wondering just how the math was going to work out if all these customers provided us with referrals and then there was 100% discount on some of these licenses. If you do the math on it, if each one person refers 100 people, you go from one person to 100 people pretty quickly. Those 100 people aren't necessarily getting the discount, it was just that first person. You may have one license that you're giving away for free, but you had 100 people that got brought in that you're getting paid licenses for. If you have a membership program, this is a great idea to actually leverage for that. An example, like I just shared, is you can offer a 1% discount. You can also do that in the case where you can provide a dollar off as well. It doesn't necessarily have to be a percentage, it could be a specific dollar off. The sixth ethical bribe that you can use is actually just a simple exchange of services. When you're first starting off in your business, you may have thought of ways that you could exchange your service with someone else who could value from it so that you could also get their service. But now there might be an option where you could actually offer your service in exchange for a referral. It may not be an exchange of service to service, but it may be a case where you actually offer your product or service in exchange for that referral. An example of this might be if you're a coach, offer a free coaching session or a coaching package in exchange for a referral. If you have a course, offer a module or a section of that course or offer the entire course in exchange for a referral. Whatever makes the most sense for you, figure out different ways that you could actually leverage an exchange of service so that you could drive more referrals for your business. Hot tip I have here is exchanging services isn't always a direct benefit to you and or the business that you might be working with. If your service has value to someone else, consider offering your service for that referral. An example of this might be that you have someone who can't necessarily use your service, but they might have a friend, a colleague or someone that they could gift that to in exchange for the referral that you might provide. Write down some scenarios where you could actually offer your product or service and exchange that for a referral. The seventh way here is free tools and or info. This isn't necessarily a lead magnet, which is where a lot of people tend to go towards. In exchange for a referral, this is where you would actually offer a free tool or an info packet that you've created in exchange for a referral. But typically it's hidden behind some kind of wall. It's something that you might be pulling from the vault, so to speak. So in this case, it might be something that you've pre-recorded and you could take a tool or a document that you offer within one of your products or offerings and offer that in exchange for a referral. Let's say you think about your value ladder and your higher level mastermind students or maybe your one-on-one coaching clients get certain things from you that nobody else gets. Offer one or two of those things that you could actually pull out as a piece here or

there. Maybe it's a recording of an actual mastermind session that you offer to someone in exchange for a referral and they would have only been able to get access to that recording had they been part of the mastermind itself. It's a great way to leverage that additional content and actually offer it in a way that creates referrals. My last one and one of my absolute favorites is just to throw a party. When you throw a party, one of the things I suggest is throw it in someone's honor and allow them to provide the guest list. My wife is actually a wedding planner and one of the things that we did was we would take people that had already gone through the wedding, right? We would take the bride or the groom and we would invite them back and we would throw a party in their honor, sign up a way of congratulate them on the wedding itself. But we would let them invite all the guests. And the cool thing there was who were their guests? They were typically people that were around the same age, around the same stage in life, typically either just married or getting married and they had an interest in what it was that they were actually providing. So by throwing a party in that way, you can actually get a lot of people that are in that same type of environment, that same time in their life and offer them the opportunity to join your product, your service or your coaching program.
